CURRENT AFFAIRS.

We hear whispers that some of tlie ** gents '’ who
have perforce to put in a course of Trish at the Univer-
sity College, Dublin, behave themselves like anti-Irish
-cads. Of course, one can well.understand that out of

~ the large number of students at the College, there are
a certain number of unmannerly upstarts and back-
boneless weaklings, who think that it is almost as tony
to attempt to malse a joke of Irish as it is to be one of
the ** Saved,” and there are others whom we might
describe as compulsory dunces, who are glad to pretend
to joke ab what is a fresh difficulty to their- limited
 mental capacities:  But this conduct should be firmly
dealt with; these students have to do a certain. task
in a proper manner, and if they fail they should slp_lp_]}

be sent down. Poor white-livered lads who have no
understanding of even the outlines of Trish history and
whose upbringing has been mean, are naturally in-
clined to thinlk it * smart ” to pretend to despise Irish.
Some of the most ridiculous shoneens in Treland to-
day aro persons who have become, by one way or
another, fairly prosperous, bub who in their young days
had not boots to their feet and slept in lofts. In
America there appears to be a sort of what we may
call inverted shoneenery, for there there seems to be.
a temptation for successful men to exaggerate the
poverty of their upbringing; in Treland a person who
slept in a loft in his youth and becomes, say, a first-
class Civil Servant, or successful medical practitioner,
in middle age, in some cases, rather tends to
put-on an ‘‘ accent,”’ and wonder, in company, how
our fathers’ livers stood the habit of drinking tawny
port ! The presumption, to our minds, is that the
class of cads who treat Irish in Dublin University Col-
lege as an opportunity for horse-play are chaps who
have been brought up on a very inferior class of
potatoes.

The centre column of the’ chief news page of
Murphy’s “‘ Independent,”” one day recently, was for
the greater part devoted to an American murder, with
portraits of the assassin and his girl victim.  And it
was ““ From our own correspondent,” New York. If
the *‘ Independent > has a correspondent in New York,
has he nothing better to do than raking in the dung-
heaps of filthy American crimge, and sending over,
with illustrations, stuff that the * Ha’penny Dreadful ™
heads “ A Don Juan Pastor’’?  For it appears thab
the murderer was a Baptist minister and his victim
was a young choir girl. Is it not a nice state of affairs
when a paper, run by the chairman of the Dublin
Chamber of Commerce, goes to the ends of the earth
looking for details of crime to place before its Irish
readers with their breakfasts, and *‘ from our own cor-
respondent ’’ too! ~ What concern has Treland with the
lanes and alleys of American murders?

On Thursday, May 9th, the Kerry County Council
appointed a man as Assistant County Surveyor, at £150
a year. There were 25 applicants.  Only three candi-
dates were proposed and: seconded. ~What are the
qualifications of the gentlemen appointed, and how do -

- they compare with some of those who were not ap-

pointed? ~ Was comparative merit taken into account?
We would like more information before we comment
further on the matter.

A reader has sent us part of a copy of the ‘* Glasgow
Herald,” of May 21st. It is a long time since we heard
or read anything authentic about that greab Trinity
College product, familiarly known to our readers as
« Mike the Catholic.”” - A few months ago a gentleman
in Cork wrote us for definite information about the
famous Trinity College man, but we were unable to
give him the information he required.  Well, there is
an advertisement in the *‘ Glasgow Herald,”’ of May
21st last, of a “ Great Protestant Demonstration to
express sympathy with fellow-Protestants in Ireland,
to be held in Free Assembly Hall, Johnston Terrace,
Edinburgh, to-night (Tuesday), at 6.80. Doors open
ab 6 o'clock. Speakers—Rev. J.
Deputies from Ireland, Colonel Porcelli, Michael J. F.
McCarthy, Bsq., Pastor Jacob Primmer, and others.
Silver Collection to Defray Expenses. Cards of Ad:
mission to be had at Free Church Offices, Mound."”

Macdonald, Two -~

So ““ Mike " has finally taken ‘‘ soup,’’ and is now one
of the ““ Saved’| With Scotch Protestants he is.ex-
pressing sympathy with fellow-Protestants in Ireland.

The South Tipperary Unionist meeting, held in
Cahir, was a great fiasco. It appears that in -all there
were only about 200 present’, and that most of these
were females. Amongst the males were a compara-
tively large number of * Saved ’’ clergymen and em-
ployees of local ‘‘ gintry "—the butlers, cowmen, dog
boys, pantry lads, who must be ‘“ Saved,” as per adver-

" lisements, we presume. We see that one D. J.
Higgins, solicitor, of Clonmel, was there. Who is this
Higgins? We ‘think we have heard his name before,
but our recollection is not clear on the matter. One
J. Bouchier, an ex-policeman, was also present, and
so was a James Graham, head garmekecper to Lord
Ashpit, a ‘““noble’’ lord who ought to have troubles
enough of his own just now without interesting himself

. in the ‘‘ Grievances ’ of Ireland. A Mr. R. Bagwell
made a speech, and from the report of the speech we
gather that in his opinion there are in South Tipperary
a class of people whom we might call *‘ tame ** Union-
ists!  According to a Press report of his speech, this
man Bagwell said :—

I hope that no countenance whatever will
be shown to those people who, I am sorry to say,
are too numerous, who are either ashamed or afraid
to show themselves at a meeting like this. ~There

5 are many such, and a more cowardly thing cannot
be than people who won't run some little rislk of
present unpopularity, but who want other people
to do the work for them, and who are ready to take
advantage of these other people’s work when they
have gained the, victory. I hope no countenance
will be shown to such people; there are plenty of
them, and they ought to be condemned, and they
ought to be scouted by every honest man.

Pity the plight of the poor ‘ tame” Unionists of
South Tip., who are afraid to rally to the anti-Irish
standard of the Earl of Donoughmore. It would appear
from Bagwell’s speech that these ‘‘ tame’’ Unionists
run the risk of being boycotted. What other construc-
tion than a suggestion to boycott them can be placed
on the man Bagwell’s words as reported that ‘‘no
countenance should be shown to such people,’” and that
they ought to be scouted by every honest man.

_ The Provincial Bank is well known to our readers
for its partiality for the “ Saved on its staff. One
of its lambs, a J. A. MacFarlan, has been recently trans-
ferred from Kilrush in the County of Clare; and in
the County of Clare they are tame enough when up
against one: of the ““ Saved,” whatever some of them
may be in other connections. Of course, this man
MacFarlan, of the bigotted Provincial Bank, could not
leave Kilrush without a crowd of tames being desirous
““of testifying the regard in which he was held whilst
resident amongst us.”” There was a meeting; at which
the following tames attended:—Thomas Kelly, solici-
tor; Bart. Culligan, J.P.; E. G. Burke, John Culligan,
J. 8. Dowling, J.P.; Chas. E. Glynn, F. J. O'Doherty,
J.P.; E. J. Counihan, solicitor. ~The only one of the
““ Saved”” present appears to have been H. Roantree,

‘who, we understand, is a clerk in  the Kilrush branch of
the Provincial Bank. For sometime back the junior
clerk in the Provincial Bank at Kilrush was usually an
““ Idolater,’” but one of them was recently changed, and

The Merrion Baths affaiv is the most glaring attempt
of which we ever heard of dragging party politics info
a plain township proposition. The project originated
from a requisition signed by 30 ratepayers, of whom,
strange to say, 28 are Unionists. Of course, that did
not make a Unionist proposition of it, as Nationalism
and Unionism have nothing whatever to do with the
suggestion of an improvement at Merrion. ~ Bub the
fact that 28 out of the 30 who signed the requisition are
Unionists is rather convincing proof that the scheme
had not a Nationalist ovigin. The contention now in
offect appears to be that the whole scheme is a
Nationalist plot, and, as an obvious piece of convineing
evidence against that silly and absurd suggestion, the
fact that 28 out of the 50 who signed the original re-
quisition are Unionists is important.

Lord Ardilaun’s < Buff "’ has thrown itself into the
 We-will-not-have-salt-baths ** movement with a 1'e_ck-
less display of bluster that in one sense is appropl‘gn?e
enough when we consider that the paper owes its origin
to Guinness’s stout. “ Buff”* worked itself into
almost a frenzy on Thursday, May 23rd, over the
matter; not content with nearly a column and a-half in
its news section, two of the headings of which were
““ Signing Against the Project,”” ‘ To-morrow the Last
Day,”’ it actually had a leading article on it also. Why
these hysterics over salt water?

Here is a specimen of the platitude and hysterics
with which ““ Buff "’ tried to befuddle its customers:—

Every effort will be required to make certain of
cbtaining the majority of voters which is requisite if
the scheme is to be defeated, and each voter who is
opposed to it should regard the issue as depending
on himself alone. Very often good causes are lost
by the apathy of the few, who could have turned
defeat into victory, but who content themselves
with saying, ‘‘ My vote does not matter. There
are plenty without me.”” Every signature matters
in this case, and there is still time for everyone to
perform the first duty of good citizenship.  To-
morrow is the last day for signing the remonstrance.
After that it will be too late for action.

We were amused on reading Mr. C. P. O'Neill’s reply
1) “ Buff,”” to find that he stated that it was a young
~cotchman who was stoking the engines for ‘‘ Buff ”
in this matter!

Mr. Good appears to have been the chief politician
in the scare, and it is only n>cessary to smile when
we read that Good said: ‘I deprecate most strongly
the introduction of politics.”” That is so characteristic
of the wily old gang. In the Provincial Bank and the
Great Sourface Railway, when they have carefully
packed the staff, they are quick to deprecate introducing
““ religion into business *’ should anyone object to such
a preponderance of one denomination having been
dumped into the business. ~ Of course, Good deprecated
introducing politics into the matter when this wily old
Vice-President of the City of Dublin Unionist Associa-
tion got one Topping, the sec. of that Association, to
whip up Unionists to consider the matter. There’s
deprecating dragging politics into the matter for you!

Mr. Good tries to quibble, and he must think the public
very dense if he kopes to quibble successfully. His
‘quibble is that the scheme having originated with 30,
«of whom 28 are Umionists, it was originally a Unionist
preposal, whilst new it is said to be Unionist opposition
‘that is against it; and that it cannot be both.  We ai»
not all so simple as Mr. Good seems to think. Tt is
“not contended by -anyone that the proposal was a
‘Unionist one, it is contended that the proposal was a
business one without regard to party politics. When
Good and his gang dragged the matter into the political
field, then it was pointed out that 25 out of the 30 who
originated the scheme were Unionists, and that was
pointed out as evidence that it was not a Nationalist
political move.  There is mo inconsistency whatever
in the attitude of those who justly denounce the dragging
in of the Unionist political force to thwart the scheme.
The scheme had nothing, and has nothing to do with
politics; when some Unionists attempted to make a
question of it, it was peinted out chat so much
t a non-political matter, and having no inspiration
from -a Naticmalist party at out of the thirty
ratepayers who origina sted it as a township
improvement, twenty ir capacity as politi-
cians happened to be Unionists.  Mr. Good is a bad
quibbler.

The Dublin Sawbones apparently are going in for a
form of boycotting. The Press gives the gist of some
reselutions passed at a meeting of Sawbones, pre
over by Mr. R. H. Woods, President of the Royal Col-
lege of Surgeons. Evidently Woods is more or less
the Jim Larkin of the Sawbones. Sowe Sawbones’
ion is to fix a scale of pay for Dublin practi-
i e officers to Benefit societies, and if any

=i s not toe the line, Sawbones must resign, «r
clse he is a blackleg. A ‘“ Black List,” that is a list ot a
sort of Blacklegs is to be made up, and this is to be pub-
lished in the Medical Colleges, and those on the list,
it appears, are to be boycotted; cerfainly R. Woods is
running J. Larkin rather close. The next we will hear
is the weekly publication of *‘ The Irish Sawbones.”’

This attitude on the part of these medical practi-
tioners—we jib at calling them doctors—strikes us as
most impudent.  Medical men in Treland have not
much of a tradition; many of them werzs sent to be
““ docthors ’’ so as to relieve the half-slavish and utterly
silly and unjustifiable alleged stigma of contact with
the farming class—why farming is the most honour-
able, useful and, should be, most highly cultivated
and complex of professions. As a class—there are

in Ireland are a shoneen and nation-killiug pacl; as for
being healers—what do many of them lkow? ~ Why
there are lots of them that one would nov call into his
cat, il pussy had a cough. Ircland owes very little
{o its medical men, and if medical men want to dictate
terms to Treland, they may be w ing a dog thatmay
be more favourable to then if left in a state of slumber.

An anonymous person who calls himself “A
Nationalist ~* writes in Murphy’s “ Ha'penny Dread-
ful,” suggesting names for the nominated Senate.
Tolerance proving and consideration for the hEmpire is
all the rage to-day. Candidly we don’t care a big D
about the Empire—it does not interest us in the least :
we are neither anxious to smash it mor to save it. -The
‘* Independent’s’’ correspondent’s suggestions .are
partly amusing.  Of course, Castlcbown, the eminent
I'veemason, is suggested for the Senate, so is the old
fogey, Clonbrock, and Rossmore ; and Iveagh, the Bung-
man ; Monteagle, one of the hairy-fairies; Sir Algernon
Coote ; a J. Peacock, whoever he is; Percy Bernard, J.
Jameson, presumably the Bungman of that name; R.
H. Beamish, presumably of the rotten Cork firm of
Beamish and Crawsick; McMordie, we suppose, the
anti-Irish Mayor of Belfast, of that name; Sir James
Henderson, the anti-Irish and bigot owner of one of the
papers in Belfast. Why not give it all to them? Why
not suggest that for the first hundred years of Home
Rule, that the Orange Lodges elect not only the Scnate,.
but the Parliament? One of the few alleged National-
ists suggested by this correspondent of vhe *‘ Indepen-
dent *’ is its owner, William Martin Murphy—a fat
Nationalist. ~ Why he is such a narrow-minded Irish-
man that his drapery shop, Clery’s, recently imported
a buyer from London. This humbug is being carried
a little too far, even in half-slave Ireland. In another
column our always witty contributor, *“ Avis,”” hits off
one phase of it in his own inimitable styie. After all
Athis is Ireland, and our concern is for Ireland, not for
empires, for Imperial Parliaments, for anti-Irish bigots
like Henderson, of Belfast, or Freemasons like Castle-
town. Now that there is a sporting chance of getting
Home Rule, some of our more than half-slaves appear
to be nervous of taking it!

“A Kerryman” writes:—'* Caherdanicl Irish Col-
lege.—I notice with much pleasure in your advertise-
ment columns this week that we are to have an
addition to our Trish Colleges this year. If the Trish
Language movement is to succeed it will be on the
strength with which our efforts are pressed home in the
few real strongholds which still remain in the country.
Caherdaniel, where the new College is to be situated,
is just a splendid centre, around which are to be found
over 20,000 native speakers, about 1,000 of whom know
no English.  Surely this is the sort of district that the
efforts of Irish Irelanders skould be dirccted to. Caher-
daniel, too, is an ideal place for spending a holiday.
Adjacent to it is the great Liberator’s birth-place,
Derrynane Abbey, and anybody desiring a variety of
scenery, mountain, river, lake, or island, will find plenty
around. It is situated on the sea-coast, and so stu-
dents who like spending a holiday near the sea will find
enjoyment to their heart’s content there.. That the
interests of the Irish language will be well looked after,
it is sufficient to point out that the headmaster is
Donncav O Lomgpis, Professor of Irish, Blackrock Col-
lege, Dublin. The College will be open in July and
August, and full particulars can be had from Paopais
O Sésgoas, hon. secretary.”

are frequently published, and they make amusing read-
ing. We suppose the extracts from some reports by
sehool inspectors would rarely be as amusing : and when
amusing the humour would be unconscious. e Al
Manager *’ writes us:—*‘ Dear Mr. Editor,—I enclose
a report of head inspector of National schools. How
it can be of any use to manager or teacher I can’t see,
nor why the Commissioners allow such involved and
ridiculous reports to be made.”’

the school.—Tlie children ~should be thrown

The All for I. or representatives of whab is left of it, .
held an oratorical shindy in Cork on Saturday. Half an
hour after the time announced for the proceedings to
commence, we read that the City Hall, Cork—not a
very large building—was only half full, and probably
in anticipation of the thing turning out a cold business
needing artificial stimulants other than William’s
screeches, a drinking bar was provided in one of the
passages.

Does anyone mind screeching William now ? He is
of some use to the Carion Crows and other bitter
enemies of Ireland, but even that side of his
perverted importance has slumped in sympathy
with the slump of the Carrion Crow order of
campaigning in  England. We only glanced
through  William’s ** magnificent ’*  speech. We
see he is upset at the difficulty of finding money
in the future under Home Rule for ‘‘ such great works
of national development’ as, amongst ovhers, ‘‘ the
nationalisation of railways.” We wonder what does
William Martin Murphy’s *‘ Independent '’ think of
that item! What is wrong with poor Willie now is
that a practical Home Rule Bill has been proposed in
spite of all his antics. Secreeching Willie has the poli-
tical mind of an out and out militant Enghsh suffrag-
ette, and it is quite as impossible to take him ration-
ally. We are quite sure that if he had a chance of
doing a food strike that the civilized and uncivilized
world would be harrowed by accounts of attempts to
compulsorily feed this sereeching bearded baby. Willie
is quite ready to * forget and forgive all past blunders’
—of course he means other persons’ blunders, as he
never made any of his own. Poor Willie!

~ ; One of the most comical of O’Brienites, Mr. Tim

Healy, is supposed to be a great Catholic public man,
though what he ever did to have gained him that repu-
tation we are at a loss to know. He opened his re-
marks at the All for T. by an attack on an ecclesiastical
order forbidding Catholic clergymen to mix up in the .
insensate turmoil of this All against Ireland Confer-
ence, with the drinking bar attached. Surely that is
an order that is of no concern to Mr. Tim Healy. And
surely an alleged great Catholic layman like him is of
all men out of place in dragging a regulation that con-

‘cerns the clergy into the turmoil of exceptionally low-
_down politics. <

i Treland is a bad team. Instead of playing the game,
. instead of passing the ball along with s view to a score
. for the team, there are many classes of people who

*__would play for a victory for the general opponent

rather than play in harmony with their fellow mem-
 bers of the team. Here you have Screeching William
with his hysterical tricks and his eye keen on getting a
foul kick at the shms of a player on the Irish side: and
 there are the Protestants, Methodists and Presbyterians
- who live in, and in many cases on, the country, doing
their little best to upset the Irish team. In industry
you have the Dark Brothers, many of whom have
fattened on the LEaper Industrial Revival, and who
would far rather give us the rope than a good word,
not to go so far as to talk of an advertisement. Ire-
Jand is indeed a bad team. But after all the greatest
argument that we could conceive against Home Rule
would be the argument that could truthfully assert as
a matter of fact that without it we were normal and a
good team. The greenness, half-slavery—there is
alleged half-slavery amongst South Tipperary Unionists
now—gombeening, police testimonialing, ‘shoneenery
and all the rest, are the leading facts that indicate to
us, from one point of view, the necessity of Home
Rule, and control within reason of our own destinies.
The very things that goad shallow people at times' to
‘ask in despair, are we fit for Home Rule, are
just those things that add to owr convietion that we
imperatively need it.

It seems absurd to have now and again to remind
many correspondents who send us particulars of facts,

* or alleged facts, that if they do not at least trust us in
- confidence with their names and addresses we cannot
* accept their statements. Within' the last few months
we have received many communications containing
interesting information, but the people must be very
simple who think such communications are of any prac-
tical use to us. A name and an address in confidence
is, of course, no guarantee of accuracy, and a name and
address, as all editors know, are sometimes bogus. The
failure to supply a name and address to us is some-
times very anuoying, for the information sent is often
interesting, and in all probability quite true. We
suppose some unscrupulous ‘‘ smart” persons often at-
tempt to give us a trip by supplying us anonymously
with false information in the hope that we may be
taken off our guard and grab at it. But we are rather
long in the business to be caught by that class of people.

What good are the police? In another part. of this
issue a contributor writes on Sunday drinking in Co.
Waterford, and when our readers have finished that
‘article let them ask themselves what good are the
police. Of course policemen get, testimonials, but how
many police testimonials would be organised if the

* police were good Bung catchers. ~What have the
police to do in Ireland ? One of their duties is to see
that the publichouses are ¢arried on in conformity with
the law, but the article to which we have drawn atten-
tion throws a light on the way that that duty is done

* in some places. We hear of people being appointed as
inspectors ander the Shops Act, and there are an army
of school attendance officials. Surely in” any norinal
country it would be the duty of pelicemen to do all

 these things.

contributor wrote and sent us an article on the need of

book, and this specimen of German mechanical in-

lishing firms in Ireland, Messrs. Blackie and Co., a

and well known to teachers and to Gaelic Leaguers,.and
Mr. W. Fitzsimmons, who is also Secretary to the

business goes back eighteen years when he entered,

It is the biggest factory in Ireland engaged in the
manufacture of school books and materials. It is
obvious to anyone that there are a great many school
requirements that are not made in Ireland, such as
pens, lead pencils, kindergarten implements, various
patent articles and copyright boolss, for instance. And
as the Bducational Company of Irelend is a universal
provider for schools, it naturally trades in various things
that are not made in Ireland.  But everything that
can be got in Ireland the Company arc anxious to
support. The school furniture dealt in is, as far as pos-
sible, made in Dublin—by two firms; and abt the pr
sent time negotiations are proceeding with the K
konny Woodyworkers for the manufacture of schogl
furniture. . :

Bub if the firm have to import some non-Irish googis,
they export Irish goods in large quantities. During
1911 no less than 810 cases of school stationery, alt
made in their own factory and valued at about £2;500,
were sent to India. A big business is also done with
America, and business Is being opened up with
Australia.

A great system of sprinklers in case of fire hus begin
installed. A fire in this place would burn a lot of
business as well as property, and situated in the heart
of the city where neighbouring places might go on fire
the exceptional risks have to be faced.  There is a
system of piping all over the premises, and at about
every five yards there is a sprinkler which bursts at a
heat of about 115 degrees, and bursting, automatically
calls up the Fire Brigade as well as sprinkling on its
own account.

Catholic prayer books represent a big trade in Ire-
land, and, though not coming under the heading of
school publishing, the firm, using the imprint of
Tallon and Co., have already brought out a series of
four prayer books, and are at present preparing a good
general Catholic prayer book, to be placed on the
market at a very reasonable price. &

In one sense it is a pity that such an enterprising
and go-ahead Irish publishing firm is confined to the
school interest, though, on the other hand, it is well that
a great branch of Irish trade should be specialised in -
and looked after with thoroughness. We certainly cannot
call the Irish Educational Company huxter publishers.
Over £600 worth of free samples of their products are
sent out every year in Ireland, and any teacher or
school manager in the country who may propose to
adopt their books, if found suitable, are sent samples
free on application.

creed, nor yet was it exorbitant, having regard to the
fact that the other places were held by those of the
Protestant persuasion.  Moreover, of the two places
held by Catholics, one had been an official under the

Local Government Act, consequently if the place
vacant as aforesaid was filled by a Protestant, then the
Catholics, so far as the County Council was concerned,
were practically wiped off the slate. 2

If ever there was an opportunity of doing to others
as we wish others to do unto us, it was here and now.
Well, the great Protestant and loyal County Council of
Antrim failed to rise to the occasion.  Yesterday two
Catholics and two Protestants were proposed. One of
the Catholics didn’t get even a solitary vote, the other
got the votes of the only two Catholic County Coun-
cillors in the Council and the vote of one solitary Pro-

testant.  Like the fly in amber, the other Protestant-
Councillors will wonder how the devil he got there.
This is toleration, and toleration by those who ex-

press fears as to the intolerance of Catholics when in
power. Compare the posts given by non-Catholics in the
South, East and West of Ireland by Catholic Councils,
and the contrast is startling.  Yes, this is the North-
Last corner of Ulster; the repository of all the wealth,
all the intellect, and all the intellect of Ireland!

However, we don't need to go to the South
for an object lesson in tolerance. I am a
member of Ballycastle District Council and
Board of Guardians. The membership is al-
most two to one Catholic. We have acted with
our colleagues in the most amicable spirit, and we have
practised the principle of give and take. It is the due
observance of this principle, by both parties, that will
male for Ireland’s progression and contentment. To
that tolerant County Council of Antrim we sent as our
representative (by electing him our chairman) a non-
Catholic. ~ We also elected as vice-chairman of the
Guardians another non-Catholic. =~ We had power to
co-opt’ three additional councillors.  We gave one of
the three places to another non-Catholic. Some time
ago our dispensary became vacant: a Catholic doctor
was appointed.  Not long afterwards the workhouse
medical officership’ becume vacant, and because we
had elected a Catholic to the dispensary, we thought it
only fair to give the next medical post vacant to a
Protestant, and we did so unanimously.

those chiefly responsible in establishing the principle

NYONE passing on a Sunday eveningthrough

certain parts of West Waterford would be forced
to conclude, from ‘the sights he would witness, that
there is no such thing as a Sunday closing of public-
houses in operation in that part of Ireland. More re-
markable still he would observe that the nearer a public-
house is to a police barrack, the greater and more glar-
ing is the extent to which the drink traffic is carried on.
You might pass through these districts on any other
day without witnessing’any sign of thg existence of the

It is on a Sunday, however, that the bungeries and

The first signs of the dirty work you will observe are
groups of men of all ages in the vicinity of the pub.
darting glances at its door and windows, and making
signs to each other and to those inside. Then the door
is opened, three or four of the thirsty watclers advance
with a cat-like bound, there are a couple of seconds of
hurried parley between some inside and those outside.

The latter disappear as.quickly as a flash of lightning
passes your window, and all is quiet for about a quarter
of an hour. Then the door opens, one of those inside
peers through and takes a hasty glance up and down
the road, half-a-dozen men emerge, about as many more
enter, and so on right up to midnight. * It has been
calculated that the Sunday trade done jn some County
Waterford publichouses equals, or exceeds, that done
on any twenty week days. From what I have wit-
nessed of both the Sunday and week-day {raffic in a
number of them I am inclined to believe this:

The question naturally arises—What are the police
doing' when this scandalous breach of the law of the
land and disgraceful desecration of tne Lord’s Day is
going on?  The answer is—Nothing ab all, or taking
their turn with the others in the drink swilling. The
established rule is that if a policeraan is in sight the
traffic is to be temporarily suspended till he passes the
pub. or turns off in the opposite direction, as it is not
deemed prudent to tempt him too far. Be it remem-
bered, however, that he knows full well what is going
on. A mutual understanding and tact makes plain
sailing for both sides an easy matter. The *“ decency "’
of a new arrival, especially of the sergeant, is measured
by the extent to which he permits the Sunday traffic
in drink to go on. T have heard sergeants described
as ‘‘ the dacenest that ever came to the place,”” because,
with the barrack opposite the drink shop, up to twenty
persons, all living within a radius of half-a-mile, could
be comfortably seated in the kitchen, with the fullest
possible sense of security, drinking half-gallons of beer
and bottles of stout.

If any of the half-drunken wretches is indiscreet
enough to attempt to leave by the front door while any
of the peelers are in the vicinity looking towards the
house, he is sternly rebuked by his fellow-boozers, or
by the Mrs. or barmaid, for trying to ““ get the dacent
man (i.e., the peeler) into trouble.”” T have seen num-
bers of men more or less intoxicated on Sunday even-
ings in the vicinity of publichouses in County Water-
ford, especially in the western portion where one could
pass from morning- till night on the other week days
without seeing a single case of drunkenness, except an
occasional man returning from a fair or market.

A VIGILANCE CONGRESS—WHY AND WHEN?

REAT things have been accomplished in a short
¥ time by the Vigilance Committees in seriously
crippling the movements of the reptile Press. But, we
have scotched the snake, not killed it. Well organised,
wealthy, commercial companies are in the background,
waiting for the fervour of moral zealots to fizzle out,
and ready, at the first sign of weakness, to spread their
poison abroad, and to spare no expense in attempting
to do so. ! 5
The weak point in the campaign, as several actively
interested have felt, is, that while we have many parti-
cular and local organisations, we have no central execu-
tive linking these together and giving thera a united
plan of action when any general question is to be
dealt with.
Many people who have had 1o personal dealings with
the gutter Press are completely at sea as to what should
be black-listed. The very first matter that presents itself
to anewly-formed Vigilance Committee is, what papers
individuatim shall be banned?  Correspondence with
other committees shows that there may be a wide diver-
gence of opinion as to what particular papers are, by
reason of their news-matter, advertisements or general
tone, to be considered morally dangerous. Here is a
primary reason for the establishing of some eentral body
that will give information and leading on this head and
ensure unity of action.
It may happen that a large distributing firm, which
is kicking against the Vigilance goad, has agents in
different parts of the country. Tach particular com-
mittee is dealing directly with the local petty agent who
is helpless and irresponsible, and by correspondence
with the managing director who tries to side-track
matters at issue by hinting at the danger of legal com-
plications, of the non-fulfilment of already existing con-
tracts, ‘of libel actions, and last but not least, by re-
{erring to his obligations in a free country, towards the
reading public.
The only way to deal effectively with this intricacy is
to have a combined united movement, and this can be
est attained, not by a mere desultory correspondence
and tardy action between particular committees, bub
rom a central organisation, which will deal directly
with headquarters, and bring to bear at one and the
same time an amount of pressure which will have a

SOME AGRICULTURAL PROBLEMS.

HERT were a couple of interesting and valuable
matters of great importance ‘to Irish farming
industry discussed at the Council of Agriculture last
week which would be of advantage to the country if
the larger council of all who are interested in our
national prosperity, and have any ideas or experience,
would contribute any ideas or experience they have to
the common stock of opinion.

A scheme of live stock insurance, for which Father *
Manly (?) asked a Department inquiry and report,
would undoubtedly be of much benefit to small farmers,
but though often brought before the public, it does not
yet seem to have got the attention it deserves. The
1.A.O.S. and the Department in its early days tried to
familiavise the idea. An article in the third number
of the Journal, February, 1901, explained the system
adopted in various countries abroad—Switzerland,
Baden, Bavaria, Belgium, etc.—and there was a state-
ment in a later article of the success of a scheme in
operation in France. 2

I am aware that some creameries in Ireland have
organised insurance societies for their members—that
of Whitecross, Co. Armagh, the only scale I have seen,
secured very favourable terms; but it would be very
important for the general public to know the experi-
ence and progress results of any schemes that have
been put in practice in Ireland, so that communities in
other districts to which they would be suitable might
be encouraged to undertake them if practicable.

There can be no two opinions about the usefulness
to the smaller farmers and the labourers all throughout
Ireland of a live stock insurance society, either for all
cattle or for milch cows only, if it is possible to run it
on moderate terms.

The losses of milch cows and of all sorts of cattle by
small owners are a lamentable check to their pros-
perity, and are unfortunately only too frequent. There
are, however, serious difficulties in the way of pro-
viding an insurance against all forms of death. A
scheme would need to provide for the employment of
a veterinary surgeon, a secretary, and a couple of
volunteer inspectors and valuers from among the mem-
bers to guard against the admission of any bad lives
among the cattle insured, of the substitution of a new
beast for the one insured, and of neglect in sickness or
accident, and to secure accurate valuation of any dead
caftle. Y

But the most fundamental question is what pre-
mium per head or per cent. of the value insured would
cover the average risk, and would this premium be low
enough to tempt the majority of farmers to insure ?

In the various continental countries of which the
insurance schemes were explained in this Journal of
1901, the contribution required seldom exceeds 3 per
cent. of the value-of cattle insured.

Would this be a likely average of loss in Ireland ?—
in all sorts of cattle or in milch cows alone? And
would such a rate, say 6s. for a £10 beast, or 10s, for
a cow, be value. And would the farmer regard insur-
ance of his stock as value for such a rate of premium,
say, Os. for a £10 beast or 10s. for a medium cow ?

/[ T may be asked are the Suffragettes killing chivalry

in their war of the sexes? When educated women
bite, kick, and wrestle with policemen, throw stones ati
windows in the hope of injuring a Cabinet Minister, raid
streets, and act like violent drunken women of the
lowest class, and these acts, with others as indecent
and turbulent, have called forth rough and even brutal
reprisals from men, the answer at first sight appears
that they are.  Their actions conduce to a lowering of
the standard of conduct, and it is argued that this
lowered standard must affect men in their behaviour
towards womon, kill chivalry, and even ordinary polite-
ness. In short, the militant Suffragette is a throw-
back in social evolution, and falls into place with primi-
tive savage woman, to be held in check, or quelled by
man’s brute force.

Buf T think when we look at the psychological and
historical aspect of the case, we shall find the revolt,
or outburst, of women, not in the mass, but in bodies,
has been recurrent, and that woman wins no place
where fundamental and immutable laws forbid posses-
sion; and, further that this outburst is akin to other
time-waves of emotion that have affected mankind in
groups or in nations. It is akin to that emotion, or
hysteria, that sent hordes of men and women across
Tlurope in the first crusade, charged France in the Reign
of Terror with fear, and sent a religious sect in Russia
to seek heaven a few years ago. But the driving force
that has stirred the English Suffragette comes from a
much lower ideal than that that made men and swomen
go forth to save the Holy City; or turned the French
into a nation in arms to deliver their country from its
enemies ; or drove souls from their homes to seek heaven
Not, religious fervour, not a panic that her
country is betrayed, not the hope of {inding heaven has
stirred her being, but the hope of obtaining the power
to vote. No doubt the Suffragette {hinks that when
she does obtain it, she will redress wrongs quicker than
man has done or can do; and above all, obtain privileges
for her sex which will secure a prominent and per-
manent position for women in the Government of the
country.

Loolked at historically, we can link the outburst with
movements and rites and orgies in which woraen have
been principally concerned.  The militant Suffragette
with a hammer in her hand, and window-smashing in
her mind, is moved by a subtle and mysterious emotion
that is closely knit with the ecstatic frenzy into which
the Bacche danced themselves. Psychologists recog-
nise this emotion, and the extraordinary power it can
exercise upon states of mind. It is contagious in
rituals; rhythmic movement spreads it among the
votaries, and it is one of the secret and inexplicable -
things that lie on the borderland of flesh and spirit.
The Bacchee tore a bull to pieces—in the earlier ritual
of the god it was a man—and ate its raw flesh. The
swinging of the thyrsus, the beating of cymbals, and
the rhythmic dance, sound and sight and motion, and
the pictured thought of the god in his agony, roused

Trishwomen before long kicking, howling, biting, throw-

papers to say she felt—a thrill of pride when they

of Irishwomen felt not a thrill of pride, but a deep sense
of shame. The instinct of our women is against the
violent up-rooting of long inherited traditions of con-
duct.and the abandonment of those qualities that have
been our pride. = The attitude of the small group of
Irish Suffragettes is so unpatriotic and selfish that, if
it were to represent that of all Irishwomen towards
Ireland’s long-drawn fight for her national life, it would
in itself unqualify them for the possession of a vote.
For these Suffragettes Iire is transformed into the
figure of Christabel Pankhurst, and the possession of a
vote is more important than national independence.
The violence of the militant woman, sporadic, orgi-
astic, hysterial—I use the latter word in the important
sense given to it by psychologists—will have no per-
manent effect on men’s conduct towards women.
Chivalry is older than the Middle Ages, and in spite of
the sharp blow struck on its shield, will survive.
Chivalry is more than the form in which Feudal Europe
presented it. It is valour and purity of thought, honour
and consideration for the weal, but it is something even
deeper still: its source lies in the spirit, an inner light
in the soul. ‘‘ Women and: children first,”” will again
and again be the command in supreme moments, and
men will stand, as those men on the Titanic stood,
accepting death that the weak should live. A sex-war
cannot be permanent ; woman is not man'’s inferior, but
his complement and equal. Her equality is not proved
by her violence, or by snatching at those things that
man’s mental and physical equipment fit him for—the
virile as against the feminine—but in the domain of her
own womanhood. An American author, Miss Deland,
writing on the question of Woman Sufirage, rernarks that
while she would grant votes to qualified women, she
would not give them to every woman and thus double
the number of unqualified electors. Her reason for this
limitation is expressed in the following sentence :—
“In their passionate desire for the public good,
women seem to have more heart, and less head, than
men; they seem to be more single-minded, but with
- all their earnestness there is a sentimentality, a lawless-
ness, an emotional shallowness, a lack of thoroughness,
in the way in which they approach public questions,
_ which, quite apart from the question of doubling the
irresponsible vote, makes the matter of their exercising
the Suffrage alarming. And in nothing is their shallow-
ness more alarming than in their indifference to law.”

of their numbers, who joined the mznads in London. .
